hints: Only the 8 'key' cards (4 kings, 4 aces) matter; the m-th key card sits at expected deck position m·53/9.
Stop index T among keys: if the first key is a king, T=2; if it's an ace, T = index of the first king.
solution: Among the $8$ key cards ($4$K, $4$A), the $m$-th key lands at expected deck position $m\cdot\tfrac{53}{9}$ (the $8$ keys split $44$ others into $9$ gaps). Let $T$ be the stopping index among keys: if the first key is a king ($\tfrac12$), the next key ends it ($T=2$); if it's an ace ($\tfrac12$), we stop at the first king, whose index is $1+\tfrac{8}{5}=\tfrac{13}{5}$. So $\mathbb{E}[T]=\tfrac12\cdot2+\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac{13}{5}=\tfrac{23}{10}$, and $\mathbb{E}[\text{cards}]=\tfrac{53}{9}\cdot\tfrac{23}{10}=\tfrac{1219}{90}.$

hints: Conditioning on the whole sequence being even changes the distribution (don't just say Geom(1/3)).
P(N=n, all even)=(2/6)^{n−1}(1/6); P(all even)=1/4.
solution: Let $N$ be the roll of the first $6$. For 'all rolls even', the first $n-1$ must be in $\{2,4\}$: $P(N=n,\text{even})=(\tfrac26)^{n-1}\tfrac16$, and $P(\text{all even})=\tfrac{1/6}{1-1/3}=\tfrac14$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N\mid\text{even}]=\dfrac{\sum_n n(\tfrac13)^{n-1}\tfrac16}{1/4}=\tfrac23\cdot\dfrac1{(1-1/3)^2}=\tfrac32.$

hints: Let t(n) be tilings of 3×n; set up a coupled recursion with the 'bumpy' shape s(n).
solution: With the standard coupled recursion for $3\times n$ domino tilings, $t(n)=4\,t(n-2)-t(n-4)$ with $t(0)=1,t(2)=3$. This yields $3,11,41,153$, so $t(8)=153.$

hints: Annie wins on turn 1 with prob 1/3, or both miss and the game resets.
Solve: p = 5/9.
With ρ=−1, the portfolio sd is |w₁σ₁ − w₂σ₂|; set it to 0.
w₁σ₁ = (1−w₁)σ₂ with σ₁=√0.15, σ₂=0.2.
solution: For $\rho=-1$ the portfolio standard deviation is $|w_1\sigma_1-w_2\sigma_2|$, zero when $w_1\sigma_1=(1-w_1)\sigma_2$. With $\sigma_1=\sqrt{0.15}\approx0.387$ and $\sigma_2=\sqrt{0.04}=0.2$, $w_1=\dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1+\sigma_2}=\dfrac{0.2}{0.587}\approx0.34.$

hints: Choose 5 of 21 blocks; count selections using exactly 2 colors.
Pick the 2 colors (C(3,2)=3); from their 14 blocks choose 5 using both: C(14,5)−2C(7,5).
solution: There are $\binom{21}{5}=20349$ equally likely selections. For exactly two colors: choose the pair ($\binom32=3$) and pick $5$ of their $14$ blocks using both colors, $\binom{14}5-2\binom75=2002-42=1960$. So favorable $=3\cdot1960=5880$ and $P=\dfrac{5880}{20349}.$

hints: Condition needs the first two and last two pairs to have the same colour multiset: GG/GG, YY/YY, or {G,Y}/{G,Y}.
solution: The first two and last two must share the same colour multiset. Counting ordered draws from $6$G,$10$Y: all green $6{\cdot}5{\cdot}4{\cdot}3=360$; all yellow $5040$; and the mixed case (each pair one G one Y) $4\cdot(6{\cdot}5)(10{\cdot}9)=10800$. Total $16200$ over $16{\cdot}15{\cdot}14{\cdot}13=43680$, giving $\tfrac{135}{364}.$

hints: The coin (radius 1/2) stays inside a 2×2 square iff its center is ≥1/2 from each edge.
That center region is a 1×1 square inside the 2×2.
solution: The coin lies entirely within a square iff its center is at least $\tfrac12$ inch from every edge, i.e. inside a concentric $1\times1$ region of the $2\times2$ square. So $P=\dfrac{1^2}{2^2}=\dfrac14.$

hints: The 14 boys create 14 gaps; the girls fill 10 of them (C(14,10) ways).
Charlie stays between two boys iff both of his adjacent gaps are empty.
solution: The girls occupy $10$ of the $14$ gaps between boys, $\binom{14}{10}$ ways. Charlie remains between two boys iff his two adjacent gaps are both empty, i.e. all $10$ girls go in the other $12$ gaps: $\dfrac{\binom{12}{10}}{\binom{14}{10}}=\dfrac{66}{1001}=\dfrac{6}{91}.$

hints: The median of 3 is < 2/3 iff at least 2 of the 3 values are < 2/3.
Each value < 2/3 with probability 2/3.
solution: The median of three is below $\tfrac23$ iff at least two values are below $\tfrac23$ (each with probability $\tfrac23$): $\binom32(\tfrac23)^2(\tfrac13)+(\tfrac23)^3=\tfrac{12}{27}+\tfrac{8}{27}=\tfrac{20}{27}.$

hints: This is the Josephus problem with step 2; person 1 always wins when N is a power of 2.
Writing N=X+L with X the largest power of 2 ≤N, the survivor is 2L+1=2N−2X+1.
solution: In the Josephus problem (every second person killed), if $N=2^k$ then person $1$ survives. Writing $N=X+L$ with $X=2^{\lfloor\log_2 N\rfloor}$, the survivor is $2L+1=2(N-X)+1=2N-2X+1$. Matching $aN+bX+c$: $a=2,b=-2,c=1$, so $a+2b+3c=2-4+3=1.$

hints: Cycle length = 40+4+40 = 84 s with 3 switch instants.
A 4-s interval catches a switch if its start falls in the 4 s before a switch: three length-4 windows.
Total favorable 12 s of 84 ⇒ 12/84 = 1/7.
solution: The cycle is $40+4+40=84$ seconds with three switch instants. A $4$-second interval contains a switch iff its start lies in the $4$ seconds before one of them; these three length-$4$ windows are disjoint, totaling $12$ seconds. So $P=\dfrac{12}{84}=\dfrac17.$
